questions.question: Level II framing calls PT "two capillary events separated by a removal step." What is the second capillary event?
options: Heat from the drying oven expands trapped penetrant until it overflows the flaw
The developer reacts chemically with the dye to generate a colored compound
The developer film draws flaw-trapped penetrant back out to the surface — the entry physics running in reverse — to form the indication
Evaporation concentrates the penetrant's dye at the flaw mouth
questions.explanation: During dwell, capillary action pulls penetrant into every clean, surface-breaking opening; during development the same surface-tension physics runs in reverse, and the developer blots the trapped liquid back out into a film engineered to show it. No chemical reaction or thermal effect creates the indication.

questions.question: Why must a quality penetrant keep its volatility low?
options: High volatility raises the contact angle above 90°, stopping capillary entry
A volatile penetrant can dry inside the flaw during dwell, stranding the dye where the developer can never call it back
Volatile penetrants fill flaws too quickly for the dwell time to be clocked
Low volatility is the property that generates the capillary pressure driving entry
questions.explanation: The penetrant must stay liquid through dwell — one that dries in place strands its dye in the flaw before development can reverse the flow. Capillary pressure comes from surface tension acting through a low contact angle, not from volatility, and viscosity is what sets fill speed.

questions.question: How is a fluorescent penetrant's AMS 2644 sensitivity level (½ through 4) actually assigned?
options: From laboratory measurements of surface tension, contact angle, and viscosity combined into an index
By the shop's daily known-defect panel results averaged over a month
By the developer form the manufacturer qualifies alongside it
By comparative performance testing — the candidate is processed side by side with reference materials over low-cycle-fatigue cracked specimens and scored on crack detection and brightness
questions.explanation: There is no penetrability meter: the underlying properties are measured separately in the lab, but the sensitivity level carried on the Qualified Products List comes from comparative testing over cracked specimens under AMS 2644. The shop's daily panel is process control of the running system, not material qualification.

questions.question: Many aerospace procedures require a chemical etch after shot peening, grit blasting, or aggressive machining before penetrant testing. Why?
options: Those operations smear metal across flaw openings, and capillary action cannot enter a closed mouth — etching reopens the flaws
Etching roughens the surface so the developer film adheres evenly
Etching neutralizes chromate residues that would quench the fluorescent dye
Etching cools worked surfaces back into the 40–125 °F processing window
questions.explanation: Mechanical working — peening, blasting, sanding, buffing, hard machining — smears metal over flaw mouths and seals them shut. Capillarity needs an opening that actually breaks the surface, so the etch strips the smeared layer. Rinsing chromates is a separate precleaning concern, and part temperature has nothing to do with it.

questions.question: On a Method A fluorescent line, which wash practice is correct?
options: Wash on a fixed five-minute timer regardless of how the surface looks
Wash until indications just begin to dim, then stop
Coarse spray at or below about 40 psi with water around 50–100 °F, watched under UV-A, stopped at background removal
Use a high-pressure jet until every trace of fluorescence is gone
questions.explanation: Water-washable penetrant carries its emulsifier internally, so flaw-trapped penetrant is exactly as washable as the excess. The wash is watched under UV-A and stopped the moment background clears — spraying past that point strips shallow flaws first, the damage is invisible afterward, and the only recovery is full reprocessing.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes lipophilic (Method B) from hydrophilic (Method D) removal?
options: Lipophilic emulsifier diffuses into the penetrant layer — contact time sets depth, with no agitation — while hydrophilic remover follows a plain-water pre-rinse and acts like a detergent at the surface, governed by concentration and contact time
Lipophilic is the water-base chemistry and hydrophilic is the oil-base chemistry
Lipophilic requires a plain-water pre-rinse; hydrophilic is applied directly after the penetrant dwell
They differ only in penetrant dye color; the removal mechanics are identical
questions.explanation: Method B's oil-base emulsifier dissolves down into the penetrant layer with time as the only intended control — dipped or flowed on, never agitated, clocked to about 3 minutes or less. Method D reverses the housekeeping: a pre-rinse strips bulk penetrant first, then a concentration-controlled water-base remover scrubs only the surface film, ended by a final rinse.

questions.question: Why do the highest-sensitivity fluorescent penetrant systems run on postemulsifiable processing rather than Method A?
options: Postemulsifiable penetrants are qualified with brighter dyes than any water-washable penetrant
The penetrant itself is not water-washable, so flaw-trapped penetrant stays protected while a separately metered step converts only the surface layer for removal
Postemulsifiable lines skip the drying step, preserving more penetrant in the flaw
The emulsifier adds its own fluorescence to the indication
questions.explanation: Removal is where sensitivity is won or lost. A Method A penetrant is washable everywhere, including inside the flaw; a postemulsifiable penetrant shrugs off water until emulsifier or remover acts, and that action is metered by time and concentration so it never reaches the flaw-trapped reservoir. That protection is what lets the highest sensitivity levels survive removal.

questions.question: A booth check must verify both the UV-A lamp output and the ambient white light. Which instrument pairing is correct?
options: One radiometer for both readings, switched between bands
A photometer for both, since each is a measurement of light
A radiometer for the ambient light and a photometer for the UV-A lamp
A UV-A radiometer reading irradiance in µW/cm² for the lamp, and a white-light photometer reading illuminance in footcandles or lux for the ambient
questions.explanation: They are different physical quantities: irradiance is radiant power per unit area through a UV-A-band sensor, while illuminance is eye-weighted visible light. A photometer cannot vouch for a UV lamp, and a radiometer says nothing about booth ambient — knowing which meter answers which question is Level II knowledge.

questions.question: An LED UV-A lamp is proposed to replace the booth's mercury-vapor lamps. What governs whether it may be used?
options: LED lamps are automatically acceptable because they reach stable output almost instantly
It must be a certified model — wavelength, irradiance, and beam profile per ASTM E3022, or as listed in the procedure — and it still answers to the same radiometer checks as any UV-A source
It is acceptable if it visibly appears as bright as the mercury lamp it replaces
LED sources are prohibited in fluorescent penetrant inspection
questions.explanation: LEDs dominate new equipment for good reasons — near-instant stable output, cooler running, long life — but they are not automatically acceptable. Aerospace practice requires LED UV-A sources certified under ASTM E3022, and every lamp, LED or mercury, must prove the same minimum intensity at the surface to a calibrated radiometer.

questions.question: A fluorescent inspection is performed in the field with the portable kit and a battery-powered UV-A lamp. Which lighting requirement applies?
options: Field work is exempt from booth lighting numbers when a portable kit is used
Only white light must be verified, since no booth is involved
The same numbers as the booth — at least 1,000 µW/cm² of UV-A at the examination surface with ambient white light controlled — proven with the same meters
Half the booth minimum is allowed to account for battery-powered lamps
questions.explanation: The portable kit compresses the same chemistry into aerosol cans, and the same physics sets the same rules: a portable fluorescent inspection answers to every lighting number a booth does. Darkened viewing conditions and a measured UV-A minimum at the surface apply wherever the indication must be seen.
